data.title: NRA pushes back on Trump, Patel saying people who attend protests shouldn't have guns
data.description:
The National Rifle Association (NRA) on Tuesday pushed back against statements from President Trump and FBI Director Kash Patel stating that individuals should not bring guns to protests. 

“The NRA unequivocally believes that all law-abiding citizens have a right to keep and bear arms anywhere they have a legal right to be,” the NRA wrote in a Tuesday post on the social media platform X.

In recent days, Trump and some allies have criticized the protester shot and killed by Border Patrol agents in Minneapolis for having a 9mm hand gun at the site of protest.

Alex Pretti, who was a legal gun owner, was pepper sprayed before he was fatally shot by agents after helping a woman up who was shoved to the ground.

“You can’t have guns. You can’t walk in with guns,” Trump told reporters at the White House on Tuesday.

The president went on to call the incident “very unfortunate.”

On Sunday, Patel said, “You cannot bring a firearm loaded with multiple magazines to any sort of protest that you want. It’s that simple. You don’t have the right to break the law and incite violence,” during an appearance on Fox News’ “Sunday Morning Futures.”

Gun advocates have railed against the Trump administration’s claims, citing Second Amendment rights.

“Peaceful protests while armed isn’t radical—it’s American. The First and Second Amendments protect those rights, and they always have,” Gun Owners of America, a lobbying group, wrote in a Tuesday post on X.

“GOA will hold any administration accountable,” they added.

data.title: Jeffries: Firing Noem wouldn't be enough for Democrats to support DHS funding bill
data.description:
House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries (D-N.Y.) said Wednesday that ousting Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem from her role “is of course not enough” for Democrats to support a funding bill for the agency.

“We have a DHS killing machine,” Jeffries said, referring to two fatal shootings in Minneapolis this month involving immigration officials. “The American people have seen it. This is an administration that promised to deport violent felons. We all support that, but that’s not what’s happening right now.”

On Tuesday, Jeffries and several other House Democrat leaders threatened to launch impeachment proceedings against Noem if President Trump doesn’t fire her over the agency’s ramped up use of force in its immigration enforcement operations. The lawmakers wrote in a joint letter, “We can do this the easy way or the hard way.”

“Donald Trump needs to fire Kristi Noem immediately,” the New York Democrat said Wednesday morning. “We know that she is a malignant, corrupt, pathological liar. She’s unqualified. She never should have been confirmed by Republicans in the United States Senate.”

He also noted that he’s currently in conversation with other House Democrats about the “next steps” in this process. 

Jeffries’ comments follow weeks of unrest in Minnesota, where the Trump administration’s crackdown on illegal immigration has sparked waves of protest across the state. Federal officers shot and killed two American citizens during the operation and shot a Venezuelan migrant in the leg while he attempted to evade arrest. 

DHS has maintained that the officers were acting in self-defense. Elected Democrats and state officials have vehemently disputed these claims and criticized the administration for making public assessments prior to conclusive investigative results.

Following the Jan. 7 killing of Renee Good by an Immigration and Customs Enforcement officer and the death of 37-year-old intensive care nurse Alex Pretti by Border Patrol agents over the weekend, many Senate Democrats expressed outrage and stated they will not back a six-bill funding package to avoid a partial government shutdown at the end of the week.

The House passed the minibus last week despite the opposition. The Republican-backed measure needs a minimum of eight Democrats in the upper chamber in order to advance.

Sen. John Fetterman (D-Penn.) is the only Senate Democrat so far to say that he will join Republicans and vote in favor of the funding bills. Fetterman, who has joined Democrats’ calls for Noem’s firing and criticized federal agent’s tactics in the Minnesota operation, said on Monday that he “will never vote to shut our government down.”

In a statement following Pretti’s shooting, Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.) said he and other Democrats in the legislative chamber will not vote for the package unless allocated funding for DHS is stripped. The DHS bill would give $10 billion more to ICE. 

Removing funding for the agency would require calling the House back into session to re-vote on the package — something Republican leadership says they are unwilling to do. 

“What’s happening in Minnesota is appalling — and unacceptable in any American city,” Schumer said in a statement Saturday. “Democrats sought common sense reforms in the Department of Homeland Security spending bill, the DHS bill is woefully inadequate to rein in the abuses of ICE. I will vote no.”

Jeffries further criticized Noem’s leadership Wednesday, saying, “She is creating harm to the American people.”

“Taxpayer dollars are actually being used by the department to kill American citizens, to brutalize law-abiding immigrant families,” he said. “That’s unacceptable, so she’s gotta go.”

data.title: Homan says meetings with Walz, Frey a 'productive starting point'
data.description:
White House border czar Tom Homan said recent meetings with Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D) and Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey (D) have gone well in the wake of recent tension over President Trump’s immigration agenda in the state.

“Today I met with Governor Walz, Mayor Frey, and top law enforcement officials to discuss the issues on the ground in Minnesota. We all agree that we need to support our law enforcement officers and get criminals off the streets,” Homan said in a post on the social platform X on Tuesday.

“While we don’t agree on everything, these meetings were a productive starting point and I look forward to more conversations with key stakeholders in the days ahead,” he added.

Homan’s comments follow weeks of tension in Minnesota over Trump’s immigration agenda. The recent fatal shootings of Alex Pretti and Renee Good in Minneapolis by federal immigration authorities have sparked widespread backlash toward the administration.

Frey said that he and Minneapolis Police Department Chief Brian O’Hara “met with Border Czar Homan and had a productive conversation.”

“I reiterated that my main ask is for Operation Metro Surge to end as quickly as possible. Public safety works best when it’s built on community trust, not tactics that create fear or division,” he added in a Tuesday post on X.

Trump on Monday announced that Homan was headed to Minnesota following Saturday’s fatal shooting of Pretti.

“I am sending Tom Homan to Minnesota tonight. He has not been involved in that area, but knows and likes many of the people there,” Trump said on his Truth Social platform. “Tom is tough but fair and will report to me.”

Walz’s office on Tuesday released a statement on the meeting to NewsNation.

“Governor Walz met with Tom Homan this morning and reiterated Minnesota’s priorities: impartial investigations into the Minneapolis shootings involving federal agents, a swift, significant reduction in the number of federal forces in Minnesota, and an end to the campaign of retribution against Minnesota,” Walz’s office said in the statement.

data.title: Live updates Senate tackles funding package amid DHS furor; Trump touts 'Trump accounts'
data.description:
The Senate on Wednesday is working to advance a six-bill spending package, despite calls from Democrats to separate out Department of Homeland Security (DHS) funding after the deadly weekend shooting of a U.S. citizen in Minneapolis by federal immigration officials.

The incident has put Republicans on defense, and they are starting to splinter over support for DHS Secretary Kristi Noem. She retains the confidence of President Trump, however.

Overnight, Minnesota Rep. Ilhan Omar (D), who represents Minneapolis and some of its suburbs, was attacked at town hall in the city, as she called for Noem’s impeachment.

Secretary of State Marco Rubio, meanwhile, is testifying before the Senate on the administration’s actions in Venezuela. Watch live here. Trump, earlier Wednesday, spoke at a Treasury Department summit, where he promoted his investment accounts for newborn babies.

Later, the Federal Reserve will announce its interest rate decision and chair Jerome Powell will deliver remarks. The central bank is expected to keep rates steady.

Follow live below.

data.title: More 'No Kings' protests planned for spring in wake of Minnesota deaths
data.description:
Organizers behind the massive “No Kings” demonstrations that swept across the U.S. last year are planning another round of rallies this spring as furor grows over the fatal shootings of two U.S. citizens by federal law enforcement officers.

The next day of protests is scheduled for March 28, with a flagship event in the Twin Cities, the center of the Trump administration’s latest immigration crackdown.

“In 2025, millions of Americans came together in nonviolent protest to oppose the growing authoritarian actions of the Trump administration and affirm that this nation belongs to its people, not to kings,” a message on the “No Kings” website stated.

“Since then, people have continued to rise up nonviolently against the Trump administration’s ongoing brutality and abuses of power, including the latest escalation in Minnesota,” it continued.

Ezra Levin, the co-executive director of the nonprofit Invisible, told The Associated Press that organizers expect it to be “the largest protest in American history,” predicting that as many as 9 million people would participate.

Levin told the outlet that plans for another round of protests had already been in progress, but the deaths of Alex Pretti and Renee Good this month prompted them to shift focus toward Minneapolis.

Pretti, an ICU nurse, was shot and killed on Saturday during what the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) called an “armed struggle” with U.S. Border Patrol agents.

Federal authorities said Pretti had approached agents with a gun, but video evidence disputed that narrative, showing Pretti holding a cellphone in his hand when the incident began. It also appeared to show Pretti’s firearm, which he was legally permitted to carry, being removed from his waist by one agent before another fires shots.

The shooting inflamed tensions in a city already on edge following the death of Good, who was fatally shot in her SUV by Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officer Jonathan Ross.

Senior Trump administration officials were quick to cast both shootings as self-defense, while also blaming Democratic city and state leaders for refusing to cooperate with federal authorities.

Trump is now facing more pushback on Capitol Hill, where both Republicans and Democrats have raised concerns about the administration’s immigration enforcement tactics and called for a thorough investigation.

Bipartisan calls for Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem to resign have also been mounting in the wake of Pretti’s death.

Trump pulled some Border Patrol agents out of the state this week and sent border czar Tom Homan to replace Commander-at-Large Greg Bovino on the ground, a sign the White House may be softening its stance amid the political fallout.

data.title: Thune: Government shutdown hinges on talks between White House, Democrats
data.description:
Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) told reporters Wednesday that negotiations between the White House and Senate Democrats will determine whether there’s a government shutdown this weekend.

The GOP leader has already said he doesn’t want to split up a six-bill funding package that needs to pass by the end of Friday to avoid a partial shutdown that would impact the departments of Defense, Labor, Health and Human Services, Education, Transportation, Homeland Security and others.

Democrats have demanded that the bill funding the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) be broken off from the rest of the measures, saying they will not vote for DHS funding in its current form in the aftermath of the fatal shooting of Alex Pretti by a Border Patrol agent in Minneapolis.

“I think right now the conversation should be between the White House and Democrats,” Thune said.

He said Republican lawmakers aren’t clear on what exactly Democrats want to avoid a shutdown.

“If they have things that they want, they should make them known,” Thune said of his Democratic colleagues.

“The White House obviously is open to negotiation,” he said, adding that the bill already includes reforms that Democrats wanted, such as more money for Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officers to wear body cameras and funding for de-escalation training for law enforcement.

“There were a number of reforms in the bill and this bill actually spends less on ICE than a [continuing resolution] would,” Thune said, referring to the Homeland Security appropriations bill.

Thune said the White House is willing to make some concessions but reiterated that Republicans would oppose modifying the six-bill funding package by splitting off the Homeland Security funding measure as Democrats have demanded.

“If there’s a way that the Democrats have things that they want, the White House could accommodate short of modifying the bill, that’s the best way to do what we need to do here, which is to make sure the government is funded,” he said.

Speaking on the Senate floor Wednesday morning, Senate Democratic Leader Chuck Schumer (N.Y.) again called on Thune to set aside the Homeland Security funding bill and move the five other appropriations measures separately.

The House last week passed a rule to send the six bills, which have all passed the lower chamber, to the Senate as one package.

But Schumer pointed out that the House initially passed the six bills in three separate packages.

“The right way forward is simple. Five of these bills are ready to go and the Senate should pass them as soon as possible,” he said. “If Leader Thune agrees to split these bills … Senate Democrats are ready to join Republicans in passing them and funding 96 percent of the federal government.”

But he said the DHS appropriations bill “needs more work” in the aftermath of Pretti’s death over the weekend.

Schumer has called on GOP colleagues to work with him to “overhaul” ICE and Customs and Border Protection (CBP), which are both funded through the Homeland Security appropriations measure.

data.title: Kaine erupts at Rubio over secrecy around Venezuela operations
data.description:
Sen. Tim Kaine (D-Va.) unloaded Wednesday on Secretary of State Marco Rubio over the Trump administration’s blocking the release of legal reasoning and evidence backing the U.S. military actions in the Caribbean and Venezuela.

Rubio appeared before the Senate Foreign Relations Committee over five months since President Trump first launched a major military operation attacking suspected drug-trafficking boats in the Caribbean and about a month after the U.S. capture of Venezuela’s President Nicolas Maduro.

“Why is the administration and the majority in the Senate so jealously protecting the details about it from being revealed to the American public?” Kaine pressed Rubio, complaining that all the legal justifications and evidence related to these operations are under classified restrictions. 

“I have Virginians deployed in this operation. I can’t answer their families’ questions.”

Kaine criticized the administration’s justifications of counter-narcotics operations in the Caribbean, where concerns have been raised of the U.S. engaging in war crimes for ordering additional strikes on survivors of destroyed boats. 

“I can’t share with you the grim details of the murder of shipwrecked survivors in open waters that we all know because we’ve seen the videos, and we’ve questioned the US military officials involved about legality, because the administration will not release that publicly,” Kaine said. 

“They hid the second strike that killed struggling shipwreck survivors even from Congress for nearly three months, but I can’t really talk to you about it,” he continued.

Sen. Jeff Merkley (D-Oregon) joined Kaine’s criticisms, saying that Rubio should commit to brief the committee every six weeks, arguing the administration is hiding its rationale because it’s “embarrassed.” 

“I also feel like the strategy of keeping, basically, these very weak arguments secret… you will obviously be incredibly embarrassed that those were public,” Merkley said.

“And let’s be clear about what has gone on. We had a dictator we didn’t like. We replaced it with a dictator we think we can manipulate.”

Rubio is defending the administration’s operation to capture Maduro as extricating an indicted drug trafficker, and putting its support behind Maduro deputy Delcy Rodriguez, the interim president, as favoring stability in the country, with the ultimate goal of a transition to a democratic government. 

Rubio compared Venezuela to a patient with a broken leg and ruptured spleen, saying the administration was focused on life-saving care first.

“Even when you deal with a broken leg, there’s going to be a period of rehabilitation,” he added.

data.title: Trump's plan to cap credit rates is good — if it's not just talk
data.description:
President Trump, under pressure to bring down the painfully high cost of living, announced at Davos that he is calling on Congress to cap credit card interest rates at 10 percent for one year. 

This proposal didn’t come out of the blue: During the 2024 campaign, one of his biggest campaign promises centered around a 10 percent cap on credit card interest rates. Economically speaking, it was one of the most significant proposals Trump rolled out as a candidate. In comparison, his “no tax on tips” policy, enacted as part of the Republican tax package, will save people $10 billion in 2026. The 10 percent cap would save $100 billion.  

Its money-saving potential is reflected in public sentiment: recent polling shows that 88 percent of Democrats, 79 percent of Republicans and 78 percent of independents support capping credit card interest.

Until a couple weeks ago, Trump had made no meaningful moves to put the 10 percent cap into action, even though bipartisan bills in the House and Senate already exist to follow through on the proposal. In fact, he has been more friendly to the big credit card banks than to regular Americans. For example, he attempted to shutter the very agency that makes sure that Wall Street follows the law; signed a law to make sure banks could keep charging hefty overdraft fees; and worked to shield big banks from a Biden-era rule that would have stopped profiteering late fees on credit cards, costing Americans billions every year.  

Nonetheless, a good idea is a good idea. But you wouldn’t think it if you’ve been paying attention to the pundits.  

When Trump first talked about capping credit card interest on the campaign trail, it was roundly criticized, with the basic argument that any decrease in interest would necessarily mean people would lose access to credit cards. As the argument goes, because rates are set higher for higher-risk customers to account for their higher likelihood of defaulting, any cap on interest necessarily cuts off credit to those higher-risk customers. This universal reaction that “access to credit would dry up” was sold as the unanimous expert assessment. 

Those pundits didn’t look at any data at all, let alone credit card data. They didn’t account for the unique features of the credit card market, including that, unlike in any other lending markets, half the bank’s revenue doesn’t even come from interest. And they didn’t look at how much of a cut in interest revenue credit card banks could absorb in their profit margins.

When you actually dig into the numbers, these sky-is-falling claims fail to hold up. The basic reason is that the credit card market makes huge profit margins off of everyone. They make big profits from people with good credit, and truly astonishing profits from people with bad credit — much more than is necessary to cover the risk of people not repaying.  

In fact, the profits on subprime credit cards, even after accounting for higher defaults, is more than 1,000 percent the average profits that banks make on everything else they do. Banks make six times more profit on credit cards than on everything else. That’s a huge amount of fat to cut before banks would need to start closing accounts — so much that a 10 percent cap would barely require any reduction in credit card lending. 

The only real downside is that people with a FICO score below about 760 would start seeing fewer rewards. But that reduction would pale in comparison to the three to four times more in savings those same consumers would receive in saved interest.

Why are banks’ profit margins from credit cards so high? Shouldn’t competing banks be charging less in interest to steal customers away from competitors?  

The answer is yes; small banks and credit unions typically charge much lower rates than large banks. But the simple truth is that nobody shops based on interest rates because it is impossible to do so. Rates are set based on an individual’s credit score, so rates cannot be advertised. Instead, banks compete against one another with rewards programs and increasingly excessive ad spending, which drive rates up even more. This means there is no competitive pressure on the rate. Add to that the fact that banks also charge interchange fees to merchants, and you get a massive amount of excess profit. 

Of course, capping interest on credit cards is not a magic trick that will make life more affordable. But nothing is. The only way out of the cost-of-living crisis is multiple different policies that combine to lower costs for working and middle-class Americans. Common-sense, bipartisan legislation to save Americans $100 billion is a good start.  

Brian Shearer is director of Competition and Regulatory Policy at the Vanderbilt Policy Accelerator.

data.title: Bowser says she appreciates city's patience as DC struggles to dig out of snow
data.description:
Washington, D.C., Mayor Muriel Bowser (D) on Tuesday thanked residents for dealing with the District’s response to Winter Storm Fern, which brought about 7 inches of snow to parts of the nation’s capital. 

“This winter storm was complex, and our response tactics have had to evolve. Ice, sleet, and snow—combined with persistent, below-freezing temperatures—have made clearing efforts particularly difficult,” Bowser wrote in a post on the social platform X.

“Most main roads in DC are now passable. Side and residential roads are our focus. DC, we appreciate your patience—our teams will be out until the work is done,” she added.

Residents have struggled to walk on sidewalks, drive on roads and take a scooter to local metro stops amid the freezing conditions. Federal offices were shuttered on Monday and employees were advised by the Office of Personnel Management to work from home.

The Metro also delayed bus service on Monday with drivers starting their routes at 8 a.m. EST.

In total, Reagan National Airport, located right outside of D.C. in Arlington, Va., recorded more than 6 inches of snow, according to local outlet Fox 5.

Dulles International Airport in Chantilly, Va., saw 7 inches while Baltimore/Washington International Thurgood Marshall Airport faced more than 11 inches of snowfall, per the outlet.

The harsh weather halted travelers nationwide with about 12,000 delays and 4,600 cancellations as of Monday morning, according to FlightAware.

Passengers were sidelined by major airlines including Delta, American and Southwest.

data.title: Epstein files expected to be released 'in the near term': DOJ
data.description:
The Department of Justice (DOJ) on Tuesday told federal judges that they expect to release additional files related to convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein “in the near term.”

Attorney General Pam Bondi, Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche and U.S. Attorney for the Southern District of New York Jay Clayton provided the update in a court filing to Judges Richard Berman and Paul Engelmayer of the Southern District of New York, adding that they could not provide an exact date due to the “ongoing process” of “quality control checks” and identifying names of victims to be redacted.

The DOJ reviewed “millions” of documents, which the department said was a “substantial undertaking” given the amount of documents to review and because “the capabilities of the document management systems are not in themselves sufficient to ensure victim identifying information is redacted,” the court filing reads.

Reviewing the documents required electronically and manually searching for “hundreds of self-identified victims as well as individuals, out of an abundance of caution, the Department itself believes are or may be victims based on the information available to the Department,” DOJ leaders wrote. The DOJ also worked with victims and their lawyers “to ensure that the anticipated productions identify and redact victim-identifying information,” in compliance with the Epstein Files Transparency Act.

The court filing adds that DOJ “are working diligently to complete this review as expeditiously as possible without compromising victims’ privacy so that its production can be complete.”

The DOJ’s filed on Epstein are comprised of photos, notes, video, audio and other documents. The files were set to all be released 30 days after President Trump signed the Epstein Files Transparency Act into law on Dec. 19. A portal was launched on the DOJ’s website, with a webpage called the “Epstein Library.”

Documents were slowly released in batches on Dec. 19 and in some of the days that followed, drawing outrage from the bill’s sponsors, Reps. Thomas Massie (R-Ky.) and Ro Khanna (D-Calif.), along with other lawmakers. Massie accused Bondi of “grossly” violating the deadline in releasing all the files, with the partial release showing several heavily redacted documents.

“Unfortunately, today’s document release by @AGPamBondi and @DAGToddBlanche grossly fails to comply with both the spirit and the letter of the law that @realDonaldTrump signed just 30 days ago,” Massie posted on the social platform X on Dec. 19. 

Massie and Khanna threatened to hold Bondi in “inherent contempt” over the partial release. They requested Engelmayer’s approval to appoint a special master to oversee the DOJ’s work to release the documents and add pressure on the department, but last week Engelmayer denied their request.

Blanche said earlier this month that the DOJ had over 2 million documents to review, though Reuters reported that 5.2 million documents were still in need of review.

Americans have largely disapproved of the DOJ’s handling of the Epstein files, with 6 percent saying they were satisfied, according to a CNN survey released last week.
